WoodShell RPi Cases — Design Report

Handcrafted wooden enclosures for Raspberry Pi single-board computers, combining natural aesthetics with practical thermal and access features. Designed for home labs, maker projects, media centers, and desktop deployments.  ·  Generated 2026-04-06 22:40

Design Goal

best thermal performance and craft quality at moderate unit cost for a permanent, versatile Pi 5 desktop or wall-mounted deployment

Constraints: ventilation must be 'Honeycomb top panel' or 'Laser-cut side slots' to satisfy fan_mount and thermal requirements, joinery_method must be 'Brass M3 screws and standoffs' to support both wall_mount_bracket and display_mount structural loads, gpio_access must not be 'Full top GPIO open lid' if display_mount is true, as both features compete for the top panel, pi_model_compatibility must be 'Pi 5' to target the broadest current real-world use case with the highest thermal demand, ventilation must not be 'Solid (no vents)' if fan_mount is true

Scores

thermal_performance
8/10
craft_quality
8/10
unit_cost
4/10

A well-constrained, feature-rich Pi 5 enclosure with excellent thermal architecture and premium craft appeal, but its high BOM part count, Noctua fan inclusion, and factory lacquer finishing push per-unit cost significantly above the moderate target, making it better suited to a premium retail or gifting tier than a cost-optimised deployment.

Issues

• Honeycomb top panel is the sole primary ventilation path; with the fan exhausting upward and intake relying only on the front panel laser-cut slots, airflow path length through the enclosure is moderate but not optimised — adding side slot ventilation would further reduce thermal resistance under sustained Pi 5 load.
• GPIO access is a rear-panel side slot cutout, which may restrict ribbon cable bend radius and limit accessibility when the unit is wall-mounted flush against a surface.
• Bamboo keyhole wall-mount bracket is laser-cut from 6mm bamboo stock; under sustained vibration or heavy peripheral loading, bamboo keyhole brackets may delaminate at the slot — a metal reinforcement insert would improve long-term reliability.
• BOM part count is high (20 line items) and includes multiple brass standoff variants, a Noctua fan, and UV-cured lacquer factory finish, pushing per-unit cost well above moderate — BOM complexity and premium components conflict with the 'moderate unit cost' goal.
• UV-cured lacquer on bamboo provides excellent durability but requires factory application equipment, adding non-trivial finishing labor and capital cost per unit compared to oil or wax finishes.
• Display mount tilt bracket uses bamboo as structural pivot material; repeated tilt adjustment cycles may cause wear or cracking at the laser-cut pivot slots over time — a metal pivot pin or grommet insert is advisable.

Selected Configuration

pi_model_compatibilityPi 5
finishUV-Cured Lacquer
ventilationHoneycomb top panel
fan_mountTrue
gpio_accessSide slot cutout
display_mountTrue
wall_mount_bracketTrue

Product Render

Product Variants

Maker Open — An affordable, prototyping-friendly case with full GPIO access and good airflow, ideal for tinkerers who need constant pin access during development.
wood_species=Baltic Birch Plywood, pi_model_compatibility=Pi 4B, finish=Raw/Unfinished, ventilation=Laser-cut side slots
Desktop Studio — A polished, finished case for a permanent desktop setup with active cooling and a display, suitable for a media center or productivity Pi 5 station.
wood_species=Bamboo, pi_model_compatibility=Pi 5, finish=Beeswax Polish, ventilation=Honeycomb top panel
Heirloom Wall Server — A premium, hand-finished walnut enclosure designed for a permanent wall-mounted always-on server or home automation hub with a refined, furniture-grade appearance.
wood_species=Walnut, pi_model_compatibility=Pi 4B, finish=Linseed Oil, ventilation=Laser-cut side slots
